title: Character Set Mappings
text: Character Set Mappings In principle, each Uniface font can contain characters from any of the defined character sets. In practice, however, an entire character set is often mapped to a single font. To aid this mapping, Uniface provides functions which map an entire character set in one step, without explicitly defining each character. ^CHR{/8} FONT_ n Mnemonics Mnemonic Explanation ^CHR Maps an entire seven-bit character set, that is, ^32 through ^126 to a Uniface font. ^CHR/8 Maps an entire eight-bit character set, that is, ^160 through ^254 to a Uniface font. Arguments FONT_ n —Uniface meta character set (font); allowed values are 0 through 7. If omitted, the next sequential font number is assigned. Description Mappings such as Escape S to ^STORE work well if you only want to use function keys. However, if you want to use alphanumeric keys (for entering data and so on), you need to define the translation to be performed when the keyboard code for an alphanumeric character is generated by the keyboard. You can do this individually, but it is very laborious. For example: a a Define lower case a b b Define lower case b c c Define lower case c Copy In practice, you can usually map an entire character set to a single Uniface Font, that is a meta character set. ^CHR and ^CHR/8 perform this mapping in a single step, without defining each character individually. For example, ^CHR is used to map characters ^32 through ^126 in the current character set to Font 3. Then character ^123 is explicitly assigned to the ‘c’ position: ^chr font_3^123 3.c Copy Add any specific assignments after the general mapping, otherwise they are overwritten when the general mapping is interpreted. Extending the Character Set ^CHR and ^CHR/8 each map only 95 characters (that is, positions ^32 through ^126 and ^160 through ^254, respectively). You can use the mnemonic ^CHAR127 to map the ninety-sixth character of a seven-bit character set and ^CHAR255 to map the ninety-sixth character of an eight-bit character set. For example: ^CHR ^CHR/8 ^127 ^CHAR127 ^255 ^CHAR255 Copy The following example shows part of the definitions for the character sets defined for the standard VT display: BEGIN_SET 0 normal ASCII set ^esc(B select ascii set selection ^chr font_0 map (32-126) to 0.(32-126) | 1.& map to vertical bar " 1.( right double quote - 1.- map to minus sign - 1./ map to minus sign ’ 1.4 right single quote , 1.8 map to comma END_SET BEGIN_SET 1 MULTINATIONAL SET ^esc(< select multination set selection ^chr font_1 map (32-126) to 1.(32-126) ( 1.$ int. currency sign ^126 1.> undefined ... } 2.| y umlaut small END_SET Copy Related Links Meta Character Sets Meta character sets are used for translating installable character sets and defining translation tables. In earlier versions of Uniface, meta character sets were used as internal character sets instead of Unicode. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
